transcript.transcript: Hi. This is John Capadonna, I'm part of the Bloomfield Mosaics project. It's an oral history project collecting histories of stories of the town of Bloomfield. I'm going to first collect some biographical information of my interviewee. Who is our Michael Borders. A town resident for many years. He's also a famous artist and muralist. Talking with Michael today, basically, as his life relates to Bloomfield. Michael, what should I call you during the interview? Michael? That's okay. It's important and should we go over pronouns such as: she/her/him that sort of thing. What do you prefer to be referred to as? Well, he. He it is. Your birthdate, please? 10/17/1946 10/17/1946. How long have you lived in Bloomfield, Michael? About 25 years. So that goes back to, 2000, 20-25 years, you said? Yes. So 1998 something like that. Sounds right? Yes. Okay and what was your previous residence? Hartford What part of Hartford were you from? Well, our family lived in three different locations in Hartford the first was on Baltimore Street and the second was on Pershing Street. Pershing Street is off Blue Hills Avenue. Of course, Baltimore Street is off Albany Avenue, it's near the intersection of Homestead Avenue Oh yes, I know that. and Albany Avenue. And the Third location was Whitney Street. That's what I'm familiar with because you knew a cousin of mine by the name of Steve Fournier. That's right. Steve Fournier. And what was he? I think, 214 South Whitney Street. You were pretty close. I believe. Oh, I didn't know the Fournier's lived on Whitney Street. Yes, they did in 1962, I believe. Maybe 61 they moved there. When did you move? But they moved to Tremont Street, didn't they? Oh, after that Steve when he got married Oh I see. and had his own family but as Steve was growing up, he graduated high school in 63. Yeah. I believe you were 64, right? 64. And he lived on Tremont. He lived on Whitney Street at that time and that's I believe you knew him then, is that right? Did you know Steve in school, high school, Hartford High? Yeah, we did know one another. Yeah we would play baseball and we probably skating and activities at Elizabeth Park. Elizabeth Park, right up the street. That was fun. We had a lot of fun. I love that neighborhood. I'm not from Hartford but we would visit. Our parents were siblings and I loved those years. I'm a few years younger than you and you're a year younger than Steve. So you lived on Whitney Street? No. I visited there from New Jersey. Oh, we're from Jersey but we would come up and visit our cousins, who lived on, Whitney Street at that time. Okay I know I have some biographical stuff that we don't have to go over here. Your educational background. I happen to, warn you, I researched it on your website. I asked Steve who and he said go to the website. Yeah. Which I believe he helped you make it? Yes. He set it up. Yeah. You have an impressive educational background. After graduating Hartford High in 1964. Yeah. You went on to college immediately? Yes Where did you go first? Fisk University. Yes. All four years? Yes. Towards a degree of Bachelor of Arts or Bachelor of Science or something? B.A. in art and my minor was math. But you did major in art there and I'll just share that your professional artist by trade. I don't know if artists call it a trade do they, but you're certainly an artist and an known artist in our part of Connecticut and probably wider. Is that where you became an artist by majoring in art? [No Verbal Indication of Yes] Yes, good. Is that where you decided to become an artist professional artist? Yes. Hmm, as a young guy. Yeah. And after that I think that there was some art schools? A strictly art school that you had gone to, am I right? Well I went to Howard after that Oh. and I graduated. Well I got a degree from Howard in 1970. I see. MFA. Okay. So far, you've gone to two schools that are predominantly black. What is the term used? Historically Black Colleges. Yes. Fisk and Howard. Yes. What was your experience like going to those two schools? What was it like for a guy in the 60s and 70s going to college at that time? Specifically those schools? How was it for you even just qualitatively? Did you enjoy College? Oh yes. Yeah? I wish I could have said the same. I didn't myself but it was a very exciting time. I remember those years very well. On those two schools: Fisk is in Tennessee, am I right? Yes. Yes. So that's in the South. What was it like going to school in the South but at a historically black instituion? But before that though I went to Hartford High, remember? Oh yes. And we can talk about that. And before that though, I went to two, three other elementary schools. Those things were the foundations of my education. Well we should talk more about them. Now but very quickly the questions were how I felt about Fisk and Howard. Yeah. Well the truth is that you know the education experience overlapped. So a lot of the people that I went to school with say at Hartford High and the people that I went to school with at Fisk run together. Oh really. But a lot of those people at Fisk were a lot like the people I went to school with at Hartford High and Hartford was predominantly white back then. All my teachers were white too. What would you say the percentages were? So, the people that I knew at Hartford High African-American, whatever the background, overlapped the people that I went to school with at Fisk and vice versa. So, there wasn't an awful lot of academic difference between the two. Between the two experiences. The people I knew, the people that I was a student of also, there was very little I mean not an awful lot of differentiation. I see. We Americans are inclined to divide things racially. We are. And that's what we go to right away. Yeah. But that's not all, that's not the beginning and end of things. It's just the posture, the way the value structure. Yeah. It's the value structure here. And It's not always that way. No it isn't. I don't think of it in those terms, not really, necessarily. Hmm. Along with my education at Hartford High and the education I got before that was also affected by the church I went to. The religious society I was connected with. What society is that? Unitarian. Unitarian. The Unitarians had been in Hartford for probably, let's see from 1860, had been in Hartford for about 150 years. Oh, really? And the unitarians were very scholarly, philosophers, and people very interested in how spirituality in American society was put together. We spent a lot of time listening to and talking about that. In that regard, we went to a number of religious institutions, mostly high school students in at the Unitarian Society and all of the people that were connected to that, just about all the people that were connected with that institution were white. They were really upper-middle-class mostly by and large white people. Was it, you and your family who were Unitarians or joined to that church? Well, Payson Miller, and really the minister's that came after him. Wanted to connect with the Diversified, racially. Yeah, they did. They wanted to have it be more inclusive and my parents were connected with most traditionally African-American churches. My father's father was a minister and my father's brother became the minister of the Wheat Street Baptist Church in Atlanta. I see. How did you move onto, how did the family or perhaps just you move on to the Unitarian Church through the influence of this Reverend Payson Miller? Well, my mother was connected with a couple of different churches in Hartford and the AME Zion and just the Shiloh Baptist Church to some extent. Well, okay, so to answer your question, how did we get connected. Well Payson Miller probably spoke to one or two people in the community and it eventually caught the attention of my mother. Who was a little bit angry, had a previous disptue with the Church? AME Zion. The minister at AME Zion didn't want to conduct the services, funeral services, for my grandmother because my mother hadn't attended church for a while. But that's largely because she was in Hawaii during the war. What was she doing if you don't mind me digressing? What was she doing in Hawaii? That's interesting. She was working for the Navy Department. Aha. She met my father in Atlanta. My father was a student at Morehouse. I see. and my mother was there to attend Clark University. She got a degree, a graduate degree, in social work. She was a social worker and she was there for one year because her first year of graduate school was at Central Connecticut State College. It was Central Connecticut State College at that point at that point. But they didn't graduate African Americans back then. By policy? They didn't graduate African Americans? It was apparteid. In Central, Connecticut? My goodness. I had no idea. Look a lot of people haven't really come to terms with apparteid. And elements of apparteid were still very ingrained in what years were those? Wait a minute, were very ingrained in New Englanders and certainly people in Hartford. Hartford had redlining Oh yes. and all kinds apartheid policies coming up through the years from the 1630s. The 1630s all the way up through 1960s. So, she went to Central for the first year. Then Clark where she met my father, who should have graduated in 1937, I think, but left there for a year and went to Illinois. He was a very good athlete and and so for his first three years or so at Morehouse he was the top-of-the-line athlete. He was an end. He had a little difficulty at Morehouse and then went to Illinois for a year or so. His brother sent him there. His brother was a teacher at Morehouse at that point. William Holmes had a radio program back then in the 30s, it was called something like: Thirty Minutes at the Mic in the Deep South. That was from Illinois? Well, William Holmes, Thirty Minutes at the Mic in the Deep South was a radio program in Atlanta. Atlanta. because they were from Macon, Georgia. Right. So, so my father took a few courses in Chicago, I think it was Where was it? It could have been, I don't think it was Northwestern, but it was one of the college's there, went back to Morehouse and finish Morehouse in something like 1940. So during that time the war in all its preparations were gearing up and my father or my mother or somebody, they were introduced down there by the way, and they came across a program that asked for people to work for the Navy Department in certain capacities and they looked down and they saw for copy editors and just all kinds of typists and laborers. So they signed up and and my mother got her degree at Clark, signed up for this program and went to Honolulu where my father was being sent after signing up to be, I think, a laborer or something like that. Digging ditches. Mmm. They got married there in Honolulu during the war, and my brother was born there in 43. That's how they got there, okay. So all of that time, my mother wasn't attending church or anything in Hartford because she wasn't anywhere around. Her mother died about a week after she returned to Hartford 1945 or 1944 Okay and they lived on Baltimore Street at that point. Really nice neighborhood. Really nice. I don't know it. Where is it? I've heard of it but I can't place it. Well you know when you go down Albany? Yeah well you pass the University of Hartford. You get on to Albany Avenue. Yes. Over the bridge. I got to work that way. And you go down past and over the bridge. You know where the bridge is? Okay. Alright and then you pass by Homestead Avenue and Westbourne Parkway. And the very next street is Baltimore Street. Okay, that's where I have seen it. Yeah. Back in the 40s that neighborhood was very nice. Did you know it at all? You didn't live there yourself? Yeah, until I was nine. Wow! So we were all over that neighborhood. What was that neighborhood like? Lots of Italian and Jewish and Anglo residents. There were one or two or three African-American families living in that area back then. And I think we knew most of them it was only about 14 people maybe. Was it harmonious? People of different background? Listen. Well, you know, I don't know how the people there really, I mean, I never sat down and had any ideological or philosophical discussions with any of those adults. Right, but how did it feel? But we hung out with the kids there and I never had any problems with, you know, interpersonal problems with any of those kids and I don't think they had any problems with us, either, my brother, or my sisters. Mostly my brother, my sister and myself. My younger sister, our younger sister, was born in 55 when we moved to Pershing Street. So, people would have arbors in your backyard, you know, grapes and, and they planted vegetables. It was a very clean neighborhood. I have very good memories. Do you remember the grammar school you went to? Yeah, it was North-West School. North-West School is one of the first schools in Hartford, you know. Really? Yeah, it was. It's so interesting I went to kindergarten, first grade second grade, third grade, and part of the fourth grade. A little bit, a couple of weeks in the fourth grade at North-West School. Kindergarten was pretty good. I went to school kind of early though, I was born in October. So, I probably should have waited a year. Yeah, yeah. Psycho academically, I probably should have done, that's probably what I should have done. At that point, you know, both of my parents were teachers. My mother she did social work for two, three, four years back then. But then after they got back from Hawaii, understand, my father went to school for the Hartford Board of Education right away as he was a teacher's aide. He had two degrees at that point because he got a degree at the University of Honolulu. Really? Yeah. Well, because he started out as He went from a laborer to going to college there? He was a carpenter. He built a lot of the furniture in Admiral Nimitz his office. What? Yeah, really. Oh my Godness. Okay, because he had gone to a kind of a trade high school in Macon. When he was in grade in high school. It was a very good one evidently because they taught him how to build foundations for buildings, construct dormers for houses and, you know, he was a real good woodworker. You make me long for the old days. Great neighborhoods in Hartford, good schools, good trade schools. Yeah. Well, Yeah. Well, but the trade school he went to was in Macon though. though. though. though. though. though. For the whole country even. Oh maybe so I guess. That's very interesting. Okay, so we need to talk more about Bloomfield, all right here. That's so interesting and I think that line ties in but we'll see what the connections are. Let's explore the connections to Bloomfield. What brought you and your family to Bloomfield? Well. Or did you move here after leaving your family's home and starting your own family, perhaps? Well, after Fisk, I mean, after I went to Howard, I went back to Fisk to teach for a year. And then I came to Hartford. Well, I stayed in Nashville for a while and did some commissions in Nashville for the country music people. Really? Yeah, murals. I knew a photographer in the country music scene in Nashville, Okay. You're probably not in place right now because He left only a few years ago, his name was Lawson Little, in any case, we're talking about you. Anyway, so I came back to Hartford in the early 70s and I lived in our house on Whitney Street for a while with my mother. I had a friend that introduced me to Sharon, my wife, the person who became my wife and, and she lived in Bloomfield but because she was working for American Airlines they transferred a lot of their personnel to Hartford. They moved into the old Corvette Department Store building. Yeah, I remember Corvette. [Laughs] Yeah. A lot of people do. Yeah, so Corvette they moved into that building, you know, American Airlines did, and so shortly after, you know, she worked there for about a year or so and then we met and in 84 we got married. In 84 and we had our first child in 84. So our oldest son, who's 38 now, and living in South Carolina in Myrtle Beach. He's an auto claims adjuster. Anyway. What got you to Bloomfield? Well Sharon. I'm sorry, Sharon. Sharon. Well remember that American Airlines moved to Hartford and she got a condominium in Bloomfield I see. So we lived in that condominium, her condominium, till we had our second child, Alex, who's 32 now. And they were more or less born and raised there in Wintonbury, I think it was, Wintonbury Avenue and Camelot Drive. Camelot Drive is off Wintonbury, and it's over there by Seabury Drive. So what brought your wife here? She was the original. Well, she worked for American Airlines. Okay. So that's what brought her here. But, American Airlines was in Hartford, right? Well their offices was in Hartford, they moved their New York office to Hartford. I see. So, I'm just wondering what made her choose Bloomfield? Any idea? Just the reasonability. She looked around for different apartments and she just settled on that one. Okay, she probably looked around and evaluated things. I didn't have anything to do with that because we didn't know one another then. I'll just explain that kind of the theme here is what brings people who aren't lifelong residents what brought them to Bloomfield?In this case, your wife brought you, and I was wondering what brought her and in her case, it was her job and she looked around for a place and found it a place in Bloomfield. Yeah. So what year do you think that would have been that you guys moved to Bloomfield together or that you moved into her place. Into her place. [Laughs] It was then it was probably something like seventy Because you guys got married in 84. It was something like 82. Okay. What was Bloomfield like then that you remember? Very similar to the way it is now or has it changed a lot since then? Well, we moved from Camelot Drive to where we are now, which is very different. Well, it's yeah, a different kind of neighborhood because we live I don't know Camelot Drive, but I was over your house last night so I know that neighborhood. Okay and that, that neighborhood it's a very quiet neighborhood. Your current neighborhood? Yeah. Yeah, it looks very nice. Oh, it is. We love that place and Camelot Drive, you know, our experience at Camelot Drive was pretty good. It was more densely populated than it is now. It was? Yeah Really. I'm thinking Wintonbury Avenue near Seabury is kind of wide open spaces that I think of. Looks like there's plenty of room there. Yes, oh there is. When I say that it's more densely populated, I mean compared to where we live now. Okay. I've never been to Camelot Drive so. Because it was a condominium and so we were sharing a wall. Oh yeah. With the people next door see. So we moved into an actual house. Yeah. Well that's certainly different. Yeah. What about the town at the time? Let's see. Did your kids go to Bloomfield schools? Yes. The late 80s and early 90s? That sort of thing? Well late, yeah, late 80s and early 90s, that's right. And our youngest son went to a Montessori school first, then to the Bloomfield schools. How was that change, by the way, do you think? How do you transition from Montessori, to Bloomfield's schools? Is that seamless? Is that smooth usually? Or any Montessori to any public school? Okay. Yeah, we're talking our experience with Montessori and their education's. A lot of our perceptions were structured according to what their needs were. Sure. So Alex's transition to a public school was pretty seamless. But a large part of that is because of him because of the way he is. He's adaptable, you mean? Well, you know, he had by the time he got to, you know, first grade was I mean, he just inhaled and exhaled first grade, it was not really situational. I mean, he already had all of that stuff, okay? And so school for him was more of a social experience than anything else, but they didn't push him that much. He had pretty good teachers, you know, he had pretty good teachers. He had, you know, Do you remember what school that was? Yeah, that's Metacommet Elementary School. That's on School Street. Yes. And Nick did to. Nick went straight to kindergarten, first grade, second grade, third grade at Metacommet School. And then to Carmen Arace Middle School? Yes, then to Carmen Arace Middle School. Nick went to Carmen Arace and it was still Did Nick go to Bloomfield Schools all the way through? No, well Nick did. Nick went to Carmen Arace and then to the Bloomfield High School. Bloomfield schools, understand, up through the years became more and more non-white because because whites didn't want to have their children, it was too non-white and that became a problem. White flight after that? Well, yeah and along with it, of course, the quality of education went downhill too. I wonder why that is? Well. I know it is obvious that it happens, but I still don't quite get it. Do you have a good handle on why schools have to deteriorate once a more privileged class leaves town and people of a lower income level stay or what's your take? Well, you don't understand racism in America then. Does anybody? Well now see, look, one of my problem. I don't know why you don't understand that. Maybe I I mean I don't know why. No. You could understand. A lot of white people do understand that. What happens with American society and racism, and how it works, how it affects things. It affects everything in American life. Absolutely. Well, wait a minute. See, you're buying it though. Oh, more than that. But if you do, then why wouldn't you understand why the education system would go downhill? Not why it does? I do understand that. I get that. Okay, but you asked why that way? I should have worded it: does that have to necessarily? In other words, can you guard against that? We're talking about people, talking about human beings, talk about resources, talking about teachers. Fewer teachers, perhaps, are attracted to the school system because it's now perceived as a predominantly black school system. So they don't come. I think you have to educate me. Well, you know. Too much for this interview. Racism affects everything in American life, it affects the teachers, the students, the custodians, everybody, okay? And all of their values and inhibitions and things are connected, tied into that, okay? So, why would the Bloomfield schools start going downhill? Because the white power structure doesn't ascribe the importance of educating its total population, you know, the same. It apportions, you know, the quality of education and everything else. According to, frequently according to, a racial structure. Okay. You and me have to sit down and I want to talk more about this, but right now I got to talk more about other stuff in Bloomfield for this interview. That's what they asked me to do. Okay. So this is a good one. How has your sense of place evolved in relation to living in town and getting to know more about the community? In other words, you've been here since 84 has there been an evoluiton and what you think about where you are? About the place you are Bloomfield over these past 30 odd years, almost 40 years now? Okay. Break that down a little bit. I will. How has your sense of place, Bloomfield Yeah. The place you are, how has it evolved? How has Bloomfield evolved in relation to your being a resident here? Since you've been here, was it much different in 84 than it is now? How you perceive the town? I think the town was a little bit more cosmopolitan back then. Really? Yeah a little bit, yeah. Look at the the mall over there. Wintonbury Mall? Wintonbury Mall. You know the stores. It was pretty well rented out. Yeah. You know I mean a lot more proportionately, it was busier. We had a theater there? Yeah, just until last year. Yeah. Our son, our oldest son, that was his first job working in that theater, you know. It was so nice. It was pretty good. Yeah. So, since then you know, the Bloomfield, I guess, you know, coming and going and day-to-day kind of environment changed a little bit. We lost the hardware store. Yeah That was a really good place, you know? And then we got Near Prosser Lane, right? Yes and we used their services quite a lot. I mean, you know, we bought a lot of stuff in there and everything. Yeah, it was pretty good and so And now Lowe's came to town and it's all over. Yeah. So, that's a loss, a little bit, of the small town life, I would say. Look, we got the chicken coops over there, you know, the apartments. You know, the new apartments. Heirloom. Heirloom. The new big, huge things there? They built right up to the sidewalk. Oh, I know. Did that break your heart? Well, you know, they're trying to bolster the tax. Yes they are and I talked to a, well, we won't go into that, but they want to get more people in, more tax revenue and that could mean better services for the town and better schools. Yeah. I'm hoping it does. Yeah, I know. Keep your fingers crossed. Yeah. So it has changed in that regard. Can you tell me anything about activity, town activities, that you would participate in over the years? Were you a town kind of guy? Did you kind of go to ball games here and usually do that through your kids? Town events? We have several celebrations in town, tocelebrate Bloomfield day? I was there this past year. Did you get involved in the town much? In the town wide activities? Most of our time was centered around, our time in Bloomfield, was centered around our children's activities: baseball, Boy Scouts, soccer and our youngest son went to the University High School in Hartford. Oh Yeah. University of Hartford High School Yes. Well, yes. Or whatever it's called. And so that was one of those schools were where they have a lottery. Yeah and so his number fortunately came up. This brings up something I meant to ask you before. It sounds like your family has been in academics going back to your parents certainly. Does it go farther than that? My grandparents didn't. My father didn't go to high school, you know. So, but you had people being educated before World War Two, which wasn't the rule white or black people did not go to college and your family did. Does that extend to your grandparents? It extended to my father's father. He went to a Theological Seminary of some kind in Georgia back in the 20s, I guess. While in the 20s anyways, my father was born in 1915. Mine too. And let's see, he practiced you know, all the way. I guess he died in something like 1960. Oh, he was a young man. Well. You're talking about your dad? No, my father's father. Your grandfather. My father's father, who died in 1960. He was still young. No, he was an old man. He was an old man at that point. Remember, my father, his son, my grandfather's son was born in 1915. Okay. So Mike. So my grandfather was He was born in the 1880s or 90s or something? 1880s yeah, 1800s. Oh so he was in his 80s? Yeah. Okay so and back in 1887/85 my mother's people came up here. Okay, 1885. In 1885? You mean they came North Came North. I see. That was before the Great Migration? Well, there were several, there were waves, remember? [Phone Ringing Interruption] Sorry. It's okay. There were waves of people that went North, remember, okay, starting Oh wow. I didn't really know that. that. that. that. that. Starting back in the 1860s. the 1860s. the 1860s. the 1860s. the 1860s. Oh, Okay. Okay, back in the 1860s, late 1860s. First wave. like even during the Started migrating North, okay. And it was just just more. Some of the times between the late 1860s and 1900s, maybe, and after, there were people moving North. African Americans were moving north, It was mostly anglos and African Americans, remember. Then a lot during the, you know, those years. What do you mean by anglos? I mean, white Anglo-Saxon. Oh yeah, right, whites. White Anglo-Saxons White Anglo-Saxon Protestants Protestants Yeah. And then blacks. That included unitarians. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Theologians. I mean these guys were pretty heavy. Anyway, I have great respect for those people, those unitarians. I really do because I learned about them. Anyway, and I grew up in the, you know, Unitarian Society so I understood that they were here back then and what they did. And not just those people, but a lot of the Anglo-Saxon Protestants that were living in this area from the, anyway. I don't know how we got on that side anyway. We're just alking. So, I was connecting that with Your grandfather? Ohj my mother's people. Yes. Yeah. Well both my mother's people were two sets of people. They were: one was kind of a poor Virginians and the other ones were sort of middle-class Virginians. My mother's father's people and my mother's mother's people. My mother's father's people patriarch were from Virginia. He came up around 1885. He worked on the ferryboat and if you know, the ferry boats that went from New York to Hartford, they would go up the river. Didn't know that. Oh yeah, it was a big deal for a while. Going up the river from New York to Hartford? Yeah, but don't you know there were ferry boats that went to other places from New York too and they probably went up the Hudson. That I knew about. They went up the Connecticut River. Okay. They probably took people up to Boston from New York on the boat. Well, that could be. Yeah, I mean, you just go up on the ocean. So, my great-grandfather, on my mother's side, worked as a carpenter. I think I saw one census where he was listed as a carpenter. Cabinetmaker. This is Grandfather? This is my great-grandfather. Great-grandfather came to Hartford in 1885. Wow, you can trace it. 1885 and he was a house painter. And he built his own house, too on Elliott Street. You know where the new Buckley High School is? Not really. It's on Wethersfield Avenue and Elliot Street runs off of Wethersfield Avenue. If you go up Wethersfield Avenue, do you know where Wethersfield Avenue? I do. And you know where the Colt House is and all that. Yeah. If you keep, you know, not long past that is where it used to be Farms were north of that, pretty much and this great-grandfather built his house. It was the first residential property south of Elliott Street back in 1887. They built it with bricks. That's when I connected with Steve. Steve worked for the city and do you know my grandfather walked away from the property or something like that, I'm not sure what the problem was but I was very interested in that house. What do you mean he walked away from it? I think he had some, some kind of, I don't know what the separation was, is the answer to that question because I really don't know. Floyd Davis, the son of Clarence Davis. The son of Clarence Davis, my grandfather, who went to Hartford High graduated in 1904. And he was too old for the first World War. And too old for the second world war. He lived in Hartford all his life. Worked for the Phoenix Insurance Company. In the mailroom. And his father, remember, had the house-painting business. It was a very good business and he did very well. He had a truck. I'm sorry your father or his father? My mother's father's father. Yes. Now my mother's father. Your great-grandfather? Yeah. He had a painting business in Hartford and he was a house painter. And so he did very well financially. Okay. And he bought houses, he was a founding member of, he was on the board of directors anyway, one of the trustees of one of the early Baptist churches in Hartford that moved into a former synagogue, I think, off Main Street. Where was that church? Still, it's off Main Street. It's It was just recently in the newspaper because they wanted to tear it down I think? No, not that one. That's a different institution. This one is Union Baptist Church, you know where that is? No Union Baptist Church is on Main Street. Well, if you leave downtown Hartford and you head up Main Street pretty soon you'll see a cemetery on the left hand side. It's the first Cemetery. Okay. And Union Baptist Church is right next to that Cemetery. So, he was on the board of directors? Yes, he was and they had a dispute with, you know, in their organization back in the early 1900s and broke off and set up the Shiloh Baptist Church. So he was one of the founding members of the Shiloh Baptist Church which is on Albany Avenue nowadays. And so this is the house painter, understand, and his son became connected with, I think it was Shiloh. I'm sure it was Shiloh because his father was one of the principles. It meant that they lived on Elliot Place and they had to go all the way out Albany Avenue, but they had the streetcars, you know, a large part of that time back then, remember. They had tracks on Main Street and they could take the streetcar to, you know, out to Union Baptist Church or Shiloh pretty easily. You know, this is so interesting. Of course, we're learning so much about Hartford. Okay. He bought houses for a lot of the people in his churches. He bought property for them. He bought properties for them because housing was segregated So he bought property He was a man of some means? and then turned over the property to them. Well, they bought the houses themselves. Well they bought the house from him, probably, or a certain percentage of the mortgage. Okay, so that is what he did. My mother's maiden name was Davis. Well, Floyd Davis was his son. That was Clarence Davis. So, Floyd Davis was was his son and Floyd met my grandmother and my mother was born. They had only one child. Now my mother's people were born, I mean her mother's people, my grandmother they were born in Hartford too back in the early 1900s. My grandmother's brother, my mother's mother's brother, was he and his sister and his brother, the three of them, were born in Hartford and raised in Hartford. My mother's mother did not go to college. Academics were very important to her and her brother because of their mother who did house, domestic kind of stuff and she was very interested in the Wobblies people back then. Forgot what that, it's a religious sect? No. What's the Wobblies? Listen in American society, back in the 1800's, late 1800s, after the Civil War, American society was in great to great flux and some of it had to do with workers and unions. Hmm. And affiliation with the organizations that were for restructuring the way people worked and thought. Unionizaton, yeah. Well, my grandmother's mother was very interested in that even though she worked doing menial kind of stuff. But she was very literate and her values had a lot to do with why her three children were at least attuned to academics. Only one of them graduated from college, though. Yeah. We're talking about your great-grandmother's children, your grandmother and so on. Yes One went to college. My people didn't go anywhere near a college until I'm the first one to graduate college. So I mean this, what I'm what I'm saying here, is it your roots in education go way back really and your great-grandmother was literate. Meaning she is able to read and write. Oh yeah. That wasn't a foregone conclusion, among anybody in the post-Civil War era. Well they understood it to be an important stepping stone in being safe and healthy and happy in American society. That was pretty clear to them. It's always been pretty clear to me too. Look where you got it from. Well. Yeah you know you got to know what's around you and you got I have a question and I want, we've been digressing the whole time. Let's see. Oh we still have a half hour to go and I don't have to get to everything on here because I want to get a little bit more into this. I think that's a more interesting story. How did your great-grandmother learn to read and write? Did you go to school at all as far as you know? We're talking about the latter part of the 1800s. Where was she taught to read and write? I strongly doubt that well in Italy maybe my great-grandparents were literate, I don't know. What part of Italy was it? Sicily and Bari. And where is Bari is the Adriatic Coast. A town on the Adriatic Sea. Oh, on the east side. Yeah, the east side, Bari. Across from Yugoslavia and across the Adriatic Sea. Yeah. And first of all, I know nothing about them. The fact that you already know about your great-grandparents is very interesting to me, I bet most people don't know about their great grandparents unless they've been on Henry Louis Gates's show or something, but you don't really make those connections. You have knowledge of those people and that's that's very interesting. I gotta think that the importance of education going way back to those people. Yeah. It's got to have a lot to do with the fact that, you know about them, okay. That you care about them a, but that, you know about them. You have some, some real connection to them. I find that very interesting. They were in Georgia predominantly? No, my father's people were from Georgia but my mother's people were from Virginia. Virginia. Okay so they were in the South still have but they had migrated North until the late 1800s? Yes so in the South Well now just my mother's people migrated, my father's people stayed in Georgia for the most part, but my father came here through my mother. Yes. They met in Hawaii. because remember, he had strong roots in in Georgia, Macon and then Atlanta. His brother was very influential in Atlanta. Atlanta. That's what I want to ask you about. William Holmes. I don't know if we can, we can go into that. I'm going to pause this for a second. A little bit on tape. We're talking about Elliott Street in the 50s. Yeah, and there was a predominantly black neighborhood is what you're saying? Well, Elliot Street wasn't a predominantly black neighborhood. Bridges. Elliott Place, understand, is in the South End of Hartford. Oh okay. It's in the south end. It's in the south end. Yeah, It's in the south end and it's when your going toward Wethersfield. Down Wethersfield Avenue and it's near the present-day Bulkeley High School. Yes. No. I'm sorry I'm referring to where you first lived when your family, when you went to school. When I went to school we lived on Baltimore Street. Baltimore. That's what I meant to say. Yeah, on Baltimore Street and I believe he said that that was somewhat integrated, you were one of several black families. There were probably three black families between Homestead Avenue and Harrison Street. Was there a predominantly black neighborhood in Hartford at that time that you remember? There probably was. But you know where that, you know, where that was? Understand, look, places where black people lived back between 1885 and say 1920, some of those places were Market Street. Market Street is down by, you know where the Phoenix Mutual Insurance Company? No. You know, where the, the Exhibition Center is The Expo Center, yeah. before any of that It's Main Street right? Well, no, those are the streets that run along the river. Yeah. So when the river overflows, you know, those streets would get flooded. Wasn't that Front Street? Front Street/Market Street sure. That's where the Italians, my people lived. Sure. Yeah. And they lived alongside African Americans to some extent. Was that harmonious? It was harmonious sometimes, sometimes it wasn't. I mean, you know, those people that moved into those places had to be. Can we turn that off for a minute? [Pause] They were pragmatic people? People living in Market Street and done by the river there had to be, you know, they had to live a kind of pragmatic life and they adjusted to one another or not. There were altercations, there were times when everything was compatible and fine. Everything was, you know, things interpersonally were quite good. There were incidents and you read about them, but they were just circumstances that, you know, people, any people in all walks of life have problems, interpersonal, problems at times. And so some of that stuff went on and so my mother's mother's people started living down in the marketplace Front Street place, they lived in a barn, you know. Really? To begin with when they moved to Hartford back in the, you know, 1889/1887 kind of, you know, early. My uncle, my mother's mother's brother, was a very important person in our family. He was a well educated guy. He had a couple of degrees, college degrees, and he influenced my mother a lot, helped her through college. Okay. And so they live down in the Front Street/Market Street area. Back before that in, between the Civil War in 1865 and 1900, some of the places where African-Americans lived were down on it's where. Well, in Hartford there's this museum kind of house. You know where the Wadsworth Athenaeum is? If you go up the street a little bit, there's this yellow house. It's a tourist kind of place. It's kind of a museum. It's right off Buckingham Street. Yeah. You know it's a house. Yeah, is it up near Charter Oak area? Yeah, it's in the Charter Oak area. Okay and there's a house there and you know it's a tourist kind of place in Hartford. I keep saying that It's a tourist attraction of some sort, not a museum, but. Yeah. Okay, so that area, you know, for a while was a neighborhood where some African-Americans lived, back between 1865 and 1900, when there weren't all that many, you know, there were a number of African Americans in Hartford but not a lot. You know, not a lot of African-Americans. So they were scattered and so they lived in that area. As you move toward you know 1900, 1910, 1920 a lot of the settlement went north of there. Yeah. You know, a lot of African-American settled north north of Downtown Hartford. What we think of as the North End The North End It used to be Jewish, predominantly Jewish, and a lot of synagogues there and now there are Christian churches with a star of David on the front, right? That's right. And that's what we think of as the North End, which is a predominantly black neighborhood. Well, it is now, but that's because of all of these waves of African Americans that came up from the south. Yes At different times between the 1900s and 1960s, 70s, and 80s like that. Okay, so the city became increasingly black and brown. Okay. Well, and so that's how the settlement went and they settled there, remember, because red lining, Red lining, yeah. And the segregated structure of things. But it wasn't strictly. There were some neighborhoods that were integrated. One of them was the Baltimore Street, Harrison Street, Kent Street maybe to some extent. There were some African-American families living in those predominantly white neighborhoods and went to predominantly white school. The predominantly white school which was North-West. The North-West school was a single building and then eventually they built a larger North-West school right next to it and they did all of Hartford education, I think, in North-West school. You said it was the first school in Hartford? Yeah, but then they built other schools after that. Correct. Let me just look at something Michael. I got to see where we're at because we only have about 12 minutes left. I want to ask you a couple of things. Yeah, we learned so much about Hartford. We learn so much about your history, your family's history and that's great. We're going to get some Bloomfield in there but so far this is a story of you and your family and Hartford. I've learned a lot about Hartford here too. Do you remember, this was in 1970 that Bloomfield was named by Look Magazine the nicest town in America? No, excuse me, the All-American City. The All-American City All-America City. Yeah. I was just reading about that. I even have a booklet that our Historical Society put out researched it. It was interesting. The Wintonbury Historical Society? Yeah. We put out a little booklet. I'll show it to you and it gave the history of that nomenclature and why Bloomfield earned that. Do you know anything about that? A little bit And I'll bring you what I know because I read about it this morning and it was named the All-America City in 1970 because as you recall in 70, civil rights started to really take off in the 1960s, late fifties and sixties in the United States and Connecticut. Bloomfield, Connecticut in that year opened up the Carmen Arace Middle School. Yeah. And that was a big melting pot where people of different races would get together and go to school together and that was viewed as a good thing for integration. I agree. It probably was at the time. It was for me going to junior high is the first time I saw black kids because I was in a white neighborhood and Teaneck, New Jersey and we mixed and it was a good thing. And then the high school was very, very mixed. But so it was called All-America City, it earned that nomenclature because it was doing Improvement for the town in its education. Yep. And I agree it did and that, you know, that's 53 years ago now. Do you think Bloomfield is still an All-America City from that point of view? Is it a melting pot? Is it a racially mixed city to its benefit? Is it helpful for Bloomfield to be I believe it's 55 percent African-American, 35 percent white and everything else is like Asian Americans and stuff? Does Bloomfield kind of live up to that All-America City still? Do you think? Well, I'm not sure. I understand what the precepts about the All-America City. All-America City because through civic action, you know, I believe the council and so on got together and created a middle school. I don't know if they did it intentionally, but this school, the Carmen Arace School was where kids from different racial backgrounds would get together and go to school together. Yeah. And I'm of the opinion that it probably influenced the education that kids got and it was probably a good thing, that's its reputation, anyway. That things were better because it was integrated that black and white kids would go to school together and that was supposedly a good thing and was good for the education, especially from the kids who weren't getting a good education up until then. The less affluent kids. Like you said, you know, racism and allocation of resources to your schools and so on. Well, here, the allocation was very similar in that one school. And it was supposedly for the benefit of the kids. Are we still recording. Yes. Is it your opinion? That good thing that we had that was given to Bloomfield then, whether it was deserved or not, do you still have that feeling about Bloomfield? Is it better? You know, being a racially mixed town? Well, look, you know, all those questions were raised, you know, when Italians came here. Absolutely. Okay. So why wouldn't it apply to anybody else? Because we live in a time now when the question of race is a very big thing. We lived in Avon for 25 years and there were very few black people. We said, that's not the real world. We found this great house in Bloomfield, and I said, yeah, that's a racially mixed town. That's, you know, we're from New York were used to being with people of different backgrounds and that we felt more comfortable with it. Is that, you know, is it, I forgot what I was asking you now? Does it live up to? Is it still Yeh. Is it still good? Because of the fact that this is not a lily-white town and it's not a pure black town, is that still for the betterment of Bloomfield? Well. Is America better off for that? The question is, All the whites did leave and it would be all black. Would that be a detriment? Or if all the blacks were forced out because the prices went up and a lot of black people who weren't of means had to leave, and it became an all-white town. For me that would be a regrettable situation. Yeah. So is Bloomfield better off the way it is? Well, you're trying to fit a kind of idea of American plurality into a kind of a racial construct. I am. But, I don't think of things that way. I mean I don't put things together like that myself, you know, just this racial stuff its the tag that was put on you. Right You don't have to buy the tag you know. You're trying to do the tag. Yeah. You know I don't do the tag, you know, necessarily. I mean I start off not doing it. This how I start off and then these questions about, you know, racial dynamism and stuff is just, you know, this is South African kind of, you know. I don't think, you know, it diminishes the possibilities when you do that, really. You gotta open that up and understand that. You know, the vibrance, the vibrancy in American society is enhanced by the multiplicity of the society. Not because you fit people into these you know bubbles and things which is screwed up. Do you know the South Africans got their ideas from the Americans you know all of that racial shit is American's shit. You know, kind of, this is what happened when they stopped using Native American slaves and replaced them with Africans. That was the beginning of all of this. Okay? And ever since we over here we have been trying to work it out, you know? In spite of that though, you know, America has made certain gains in certain things and there's some really good stuff, you know, going on in America. Think about it, you know, all the stuff they don't have in the rest of the world that the rest of the world, really, really wants. And it's because it's, you know, it's such a vibrant mixture of people from different backgrounds and they can lend all kinds of stuff into the mix. You know, I went to school with people from, you know, Eastern Europeans and Italians. Certainly people from just other parts of the world. I didn't get a lot of exposure though to Asians, East Asians and South Asians, it not part of the mix here and it's something I wish I had that I had known more Chinese and Japanese and people from Thailand and Korea. And I didn't have that, you know, and I have since known people from those places but not a lot of them and would have been so important for me to know some of those ideas. All of those, and just think, you know, how men have separated, you know, the women and men all the way down through from Greek Old Times, Greek, Romans, ancient history, mostly European history and all, they separated the women and they put women over in, you know, and didn't really include women. But just think they cut themselves off from all of that power and all of those ideas that women could have presented and if you start separating people by race, you're still excluding even more, you know. America could be so much more powerful and vibrant. And if they didn't have all that division, you know. That's the problem. I mean it's a big American, it's a world problem really, but certainly it's you know here it manifested the way it did. And so we're since trying to work our way through all of that. It's hard but it can be done. You optimistic? I am. Well, look thing is that we're right now, the guy with the Sword of Damocles holding it over his head, is he optimistic? Do we have the Sword of Damocles hanging over our heads? Do you know about the atomic clock? You know where the needle is? And you're asking about The Sword of Damocles? Come on. You're right. [Laughs] You know. Am I optimistic? I want to be optimistic. Okay. But but you know, all of those times when they had atomic and hydrogen bombs falling out of airplanes. They had missile silos blowing up. The one Russian general, admiral or captain that decided not to shoot the torpedo during the Cuban Missile Crisis. He was the one that saved our asses, okay? That guy he wasn't even American. It's a Russian guy I was giving Kruschev the credit. Well ultimately, okay, but it came down to these certain you know. Yeah. Military people that made these decisions. And am I optimistic? Listen, you're looking at Mr. Optimism, okay. So few. [Laughs] Okay. So, listen I love American society, I love it. I mean this is my home. This is our birthplace. And so what, you know, when you have people out there talking about the Chinese. Do you know that everything that we do that the American government does says American foreign policy is structured around these ideas that may be the Chinese these days are not in our corner and everything that you hear has sort of a negative thing. But, if you look back through history, American history and the way Chinese have been dealt with in American society: very bad. There's a huge cloud over the policy and everything that comes forward is with that cloud. You know, it's seeing through that racist lens. Yes. Very bad and it could our ruin and the same thing applies to other things. I one time sat down with two CIA people. Did you? Who wondered during the Iran thing where they had the hostages. Yes. They separated the hostages racially. Their had been some number of African Americans working for the state department over in Iran and and they sent those people home, the African Americans home, because they understood the racial division in the United States. And They identified more with them than with the white people. Well not necessarily. No, they understood the American socio-political construct. They understood about slavery and all of that. They were aware of it. Yeah. And it worked against American foreign policy. Didn't it? Sure. Okay. This is what I explained to the people I was talking to that day at lunch. These two people who work for the American intelligence. This is one of the things that has tripped us up over the years, okay. I want to get one of those things for my glasses. Where did you get that? It was 5 bucks. I'm telling ya. But get the right one. Okay, so the American Indians, you know, and then the foreign policy and so forth, these people making decisions about whether to shoot the missiles or not they got these planes flying around with these missiles and things. Oh, jeez, listen, I'm more afraid of what the Americans are doing. Oh yeah. I'm afraid of, listen, they scare me. I mean people you know the state department and the people that set up the policies with the Russians and the Chinese, oh my God, they sound really scary. They could shoot down a drown. The stupid baloon, you know, they could have halt They could have blown it. They could've pulled the balloon back and they could have studied it and they probably knew about the technology of that balloon is my guess. They know all about what kind of, you know, that it was a 401hg type balloon. I have no idea. That balloon buying kind of stuff. I'm going to interrupt you for a second because Fiona is going to get mad at me for letting us diverge, but listen it's fascinating talking to you and I want to talk to you more about all of these things, but we're going to stop to not take up too much of this tape because other people are going to be interviewed. So, I'm going to stop the interview now. I'm going to thank you so much for your time and I want to talk to you more myself. Yeah. With or Without the device. Yeah. Sure. Okay. It's been a pleasure. Thank you so much. We didn't talk about art.

lines.line: # Introduction and Project Overview

> John Capadonna, part of the Bloomfield Mosaics project, introduces the purpose of the oral history project, which is to collect and preserve the stories of Bloomfield residents. He begins the interview with Michael Borders, an artist and town resident.
** introduction, Bloomfield Mosaics, oral history, John Capadonna, Michael Borders **
keywords: ** introduction, Bloomfield Mosaics, oral history, John Capadonna, Michael Borders **

lines.line: # Michael Borders: Background

> Michael Borders shares his background information, including his preference for being called Michael, his pronoun choice, his birthdate, and his residence history, which includes time in Hartford before moving to Bloomfield.
** Michael Borders, background, residence, Hartford, Bloomfield **
keywords: ** Michael Borders, background, residence, Hartford, Bloomfield **

lines.line: # Residences and Connections

> Michael discusses his family’s living history in Hartford, including multiple locations like Baltimore Street, Pershing Street, and Whitney Street. He recalls acquaintances such as Steve Fournier and explores how these relationships formed.
** residences, Hartford, Baltimore Street, Pershing Street, Whitney Street, Steve Fournier **

lines.line: # Education and Career
> Michael Borders recounts his educational journey, beginning at Hartford High, then attending Fisk University and Howard University, where he developed his career as an artist and muralist.
** education, career, Hartford High, Fisk University, Howard University, artist, muralist **
keywords: ** education, career, Hartford High, Fisk University, Howard University, artist, muralist **

lines.line: # Experience at Historically Black Colleges

> Michael shares his experiences attending Fisk and Howard, both historically Black colleges, and reflects on the educational overlap and social dynamics between these institutions and his high school.
** historically Black colleges, Fisk, Howard, education, social dynamics **

lines.line: # Journey to Bloomfield

> Michael explains the journey that led him to Bloomfield, including a teaching position at Fisk, commissions in Nashville, and meeting his wife, Sharon, who was transferred to Hartford by American Airlines.
** journey, Bloomfield, Fisk, Nashville, Sharon, American Airlines **

story.title: Michael Borders Oral History 03172023.WAV
story.description: The interview, part of the Bloomfield Mosaics oral history project, features Michael Borders, a longtime Bloomfield resident and accomplished muralist. Borders shares his background, discussing his upbringing in Hartford, Connecticut, and his educational journey at Fisk and Howard Universities. He reflects on racial dynamics in education and society, including the experiences of being in predominantly black universities during the civil rights era. His narrative also touches on family history, interspersed with reflections on socio-political issues surrounding race and education in America. Borders’ sense of community and connection to Bloomfield is explored, alongside discussions of broader societal changes over time.
